title: IndiSegNet: real-time semantic segmentation for unstructured road scenes in intelligent transportation systems
abstract: Autonomous driving in developing regions demands perception systems that can operate reliably in unstructured road environments marked by heterogeneous traffic, weak or missing lane geometry, frequent occlusions, and strong appearance variability. Existing semantic segmentation models, although successful in structured Western datasets, exhibit poor generalization to such chaotic conditions and are often too computationally heavy for real-time deployment on low-power edge hardware. To address these gaps, this paper focuses on the challenge of achieving fast, accurate, and resource-efficient segmentation tailored to complex Indian road scenes. We propose IndiSegNet, a lightweight architecture designed explicitly for this setting. The model introduces two novel components—Multi-Scale Contextual Features (MSCF) for capturing irregular object scales and Encoded Features Refining (EFR) for enhancing thin-structure and boundary detail, resulting in a more stable representation for unstructured environments. IndiSegNet achieves 67.2% mIoU on IDD, 78.9% on Cityscapes, and 74.6% on CamVid, while sustaining 112 FPS on Jetson Nano, outperforming standard baselines by 12%–18% IoU on safety-critical classes such as pedestrians, riders, and vehicles. Real-world evaluation across urban, monsoonal, rural, and mountainous regions shows less than 2.5% variance in mIoU with consistent inference speeds above 108 FPS. These results demonstrate that IndiSegNet offers a practical and hardware-efficient solution for high-speed autonomous navigation in the challenging traffic conditions of developing regions.

A B S T R A C T
Autonomous driving in developing regions demands perception systems that can operate reliably in unstruc-
tured road environments marked by heterogeneous traffic, weak or missing lane geometry, frequent occlusions, 
and strong appearance variability. Existing semantic segmentation models, although successful in structured 
Western datasets, exhibit poor generalization to such chaotic conditions and are often too computationally 
heavy for real-time deployment on low-power edge hardware. To address these gaps, this paper focuses on 
the challenge of achieving fast, accurate, and resource-efficient segmentation tailored to complex Indian road 
scenes. We propose IndiSegNet, a lightweight architecture designed explicitly for this setting. The model 
introduces two novel components—Multi-Scale Contextual Features (MSCF) for capturing irregular object 
scales and Encoded Features Refining (EFR) for enhancing thin-structure and boundary detail, resulting in 
a more stable representation for unstructured environments. IndiSegNet achieves 67.2% mIoU on IDD, 78.9% 
on Cityscapes, and 74.6% on CamVid, while sustaining 112 FPS on Jetson Nano, outperforming standard 
baselines by 12%–18% IoU on safety-critical classes such as pedestrians, riders, and vehicles. Real-world 
evaluation across urban, monsoonal, rural, and mountainous regions shows less than 2.5% variance in mIoU 
with consistent inference speeds above 108 FPS. These results demonstrate that IndiSegNet offers a practical 
and hardware-efficient solution for high-speed autonomous navigation in the challenging traffic conditions of 
developing regions.
1. Introduction
The deployment of autonomous vehicles (AVs) in unstructured road 
environments presents unique challenges. These conditions differ sig-
nificantly from those found in structured and regulated driving envi-
ronments. In many regions, traffic includes a mix of cars, motorcycles, 
pedestrians, bicycles, and animals, often sharing the same road space 
without clear lane boundaries or standardized markings. Many roads 
are poorly maintained, traffic rules are inconsistently followed, and 
occlusions or abrupt maneuvers occur frequently. This leads to un-
predictable, highly dynamic, and chaotic driving conditions. These 
challenges become even more critical in emerging smart city ecosys-
tems, where real-time perception systems support digital twins, traffic 
analytics, and autonomous infrastructure (Wang et al., 2024b).
Scope and Problem Definition. In this work, we focus specifi-
cally on the problem of achieving real-time semantic segmentation 
for autonomous navigation in such unstructured and unpredictable 
environments. The scope spans both controlled dataset evaluations and

real-world testing across diverse geographical regions. Formally, the 
problem addressed in this paper is to design a segmentation model 
that can remain accurate under large scene variability, heterogeneous 
object types, ambiguous lane structures, and varied illumination, while 
also being computationally lightweight enough for deployment on low-
power edge hardware. This context exposes clear gaps in existing 
literature, especially in balancing segmentation accuracy and hardware 
efficiency for chaotic road scenes.
Such environments require perception systems capable of process-
ing visual information in real time while still maintaining reliable 
scene understanding (Chen et al., 2023). However, most state-of-the-
art semantic segmentation models, such as DeepLabV3+ and U-Net, are 
developed for structured datasets and exhibit poor generalization when 
applied to dynamic and cluttered traffic scenes (Muhammad et al., 
2022). Meanwhile, real-time and resource-aware deep learning systems 
have demonstrated notable success in other constrained domains (Yang 
et al., 2025). Additionally, recent transformer-based models for UAV

Gaps in Existing Research. Although many segmentation models 
perform competitively on structured datasets, significant gaps remain 
when transitioning to unstructured traffic. Existing methods often rely 
on heavy multi-scale context modules or attention mechanisms that 
increase computational cost, making them unsuitable for embedded 
platforms. Others fail under domain shift, resulting in degraded per-
formance when faced with irregular vehicle categories, ambiguous 
lanes, and mixed traffic densities. These limitations create a need for 
architectures that generalize well across diverse environments while 
maintaining real-time throughput.
Another major obstacle is the limited availability of datasets that 
adequately capture the complexity of unstructured road scenes. Popular 
benchmarks such as Cityscapes and CamVid primarily include orga-
nized traffic, well-marked lanes, and consistent lighting. Models trained 
on these datasets frequently exhibit reduced accuracy when applied to 
more variable environments. Studies have reported significant drops 
in segmentation performance (Chakraborty et al., 2025), particularly 
for safety-critical classes such as pedestrians—when models trained 
on structured datasets are deployed in unstructured scenes. Factors 
such as inconsistent signage, occlusions, mixed vehicle scales, and un-
predictable motion patterns further complicate perception. Moreover, 
many existing solutions emphasize accuracy over efficiency, making 
them impractical for real-time onboard deployment.
Several deep learning-based segmentation networks, including U-
Net (Ronneberger et al., 2015), DeepLabV3+ (Chen et al., 2018), and 
Mask R-CNN (Girshick, 2015), have achieved strong results in struc-
tured urban scenes. However, they struggle in irregular road conditions 
where object scales, motion patterns, and scene layout vary substan-
tially. A significant research gap exists in developing lightweight, real-
time segmentation models capable of effectively handling the complex-
ity of unstructured traffic while maintaining low computational over-
head. Many existing semantic segmentation models incur high compu-
tational cost due to multi-scale processing blocks and large receptive-
field modules such as ASPP (DeepLabV3+), pyramid pooling (PSP-
Net), or multi-branch pathways (HRNet). These components increase 
FLOPs, memory access, and latency. Furthermore, heavy encoder–
decoder models like U-Net require large feature maps to be stored and 
fused across multiple stages, making them inefficient on CPUs and edge 
devices. Such architectures perform well on structured datasets but 
become impractical for real-time deployment in unstructured environ-
ments, where fast inference is essential.
Current models face three main limitations in such environments. 
First, they struggle with large variations in object sizes, such as nearby 
motorcycles and distant trucks. Second, they have high computational 
costs, often exceeding 200 ms of latency on embedded devices like 
Jetson Nano (Nguyen et al., 2022). Third, they perform poorly on 
dynamic objects such as pedestrians and bicycles in crowded scenes, 
which increases safety risks.
Proposed Solution. To address these problems, we propose IndiS-
egNet, a semantic segmentation model designed for real-time driving 
in complex and unstructured road environments. The model uses a 
pyramid-based design that captures multi-scale features while main-
taining high computational efficiency. The architecture includes two 
core modules: the Multi-Scale Contextual Features (MSCF) module, 
which extracts information at multiple resolutions, and the Encoded 
Features Refining (EFR) module, which improves segmentation accu-
racy through feature refinement. We also evaluate IndiSegNet on a 
Jetson Nano board to demonstrate its suitability for resource-limited 
hardware.
Objectives of This Work. Based on the above challenges and gaps, 
the objectives of this paper are:
• To analyze the limitations of existing segmentation models in 
unstructured and chaotic road environments.
• To design a lightweight architecture that captures multi-scale and 
fine-grained features efficiently.
• To evaluate IndiSegNet on structured (Cityscapes, CamVid) and 
unstructured (IDD) datasets, as well as real-world environments.
• To benchmark its performance against standard baselines in 
terms of mIoU, class-wise accuracy, robustness, and hardware 
efficiency.
Organization of the Paper. To guide the reader, the rest of the 
paper is organized as follows. Section 2 surveys the recent landscape 
of semantic segmentation, multimodal perception, and efficient archi-
tectures, highlighting the technical gaps that persist in chaotic traffic 
scenarios. Section 3 details the datasets, preprocessing strategies, aug-
mentation pipeline, and theoretical foundations that ground the pro-
posed framework. Building upon these foundations, Section 4 presents 
the IndiSegNet architecture, describing the system model, multi-scale 
contextual reasoning, and the refinement mechanisms that shape its 
real-time behavior. Section 5 provides an extensive experimental study, 
offering quantitative comparisons, ablation results, robustness analysis, 
and cross-domain evaluations. Section 6 demonstrates real-world de-
ployment and geographical field testing across diverse Indian terrains, 
examining the model’s stability under practical constraints. Finally, Sec-
tion 7 concludes the study with key insights, limitations, and directions 
for future research.
2. Literature review
Recent advances in autonomous driving perception have explored 
multimodal fusion, diffusion-based decision making, world-model re-
construction, vision–language alignment, collaborative sensing, and 
efficient segmentation. Wang, Huang, and Hu (2025) proposed an 
attention-based LiDAR–camera fusion framework that aligned sparse 
depth with dense RGB features to enhance 3D object detection under 
occlusion; their method demonstrated improved robustness in cluttered 
scenes. The DiffusionDrive framework (Liao et al., 2025) introduced 
a truncated diffusion-policy for real-time control, offering smoother 
lane-following and improved resilience to noisy inputs (Cai et al., 
2025). DriveDreamer4D (Zhao et al., 2025) developed a generative 
4D world-model capable of producing temporally coherent reconstruc-
tions, showing improved long-horizon prediction and useful synthetic 
trajectory generation.
FLAIR (Xiao et al., 2025) presented a fine-grained vision–language 
representation model using noise-tolerant latent alignment, achiev-
ing strong multi-task improvements in segmentation and captioning. 
GaussianFormer-2 (Huang et al., 2025) combined Gaussian splatting 
with transformer-based occupancy reasoning, reporting better spatial 
fidelity and occlusion handling. LISA (Lai et al., 2024) integrated LLM-
driven reasoning into open-vocabulary segmentation, yielding stronger 
recognition of rare and contextual categories. OmniDrive (Wang, et al., 
2025) introduced a multimodal dataset and a goal-driven agent with 
counterfactual reasoning, achieving improved generalization to unseen 
environments.
Prompt-Driven Domain Adaptation (Khurram et al., 2025) pro-
posed a prompt-based and RL-assisted adaptation mechanism that re-
duced degradation under fog and rain without adversarial training. 
SAM2-based Seg2Track (Mendonça et al., 2025) extended foundation 
segmentation models to video through memory-enhanced temporal 
association, improving stability and multi-object tracking. SAM3-style 
multimodal transformers (Kim et al., 2025) treated large language mod-
els as extended vision transformers, enhancing contextual reasoning for 
open-vocabulary dense prediction.
SparseInst-RT (Cheng et al., 2022) introduced a sparse activation 
mechanism for real-time instance segmentation, removing dense de-
coders and lowering computational cost. Talk2BEV (Choudhary et al., 
2024) fused multi-view camera inputs into a BEV representation and 
enabled natural-language interaction, demonstrating coherent BEV

queries and improved task flexibility. UniScene (Li et al., 2025) uni-
fied segmentation, mapping, detection, and planning using a single 
transformer backbone, reducing redundant computation across tasks. 
V2X-R (Huang et al., 2025) proposed a reliability-aware transformer 
for roadside–vehicle collaborative perception, significantly improv-
ing occluded-object detection. The state-changing object segmentation 
method from ICCV 2023 (Yu et al., 2023) modeled temporal tran-
sitions to improve segmentation accuracy for dynamically changing 
entities (Biasetton et al., 2019).
YOLOv10 (Wang et al., 2024) optimized real-time detection by 
removing redundant operations and redesigning lightweight detec-
tion heads, achieving high throughput with competitive accuracy. 
Zelda (Romero et al., 2023) combined language-guided prompts with 
zero-shot segmentation, enabling strong generalization to unseen cate-
gories and cross-domain shifts.
Across these works, several technical gaps remained. Many high-
performing transformers, multimodal fusion systems, and foundation 
models introduced substantial computational overhead, making them 
unsuitable for embedded deployment on low-power devices. Domain 
adaptation approaches often depended on additional prompts, auxiliary 
sensors, or large-scale adversarial training, which remain impractical 
for unstructured driving environments. Multimodal methods frequently 
required LiDAR or multi-camera inputs unavailable in low-cost plat-
forms typical of developing regions. Lightweight real-time segmenta-
tion models commonly sacrificed boundary fidelity and small-object 
accuracy, limiting their reliability in chaotic mixed traffic. Temporal 
and world-modeling approaches improved long-horizon stability but 
demanded significant GPU memory and compute resources. Sparse-
instance methods achieved efficiency but degraded under dense clutter 
and heterogeneous traffic patterns. Research on incomplete or unre-
liable representations also showed that missing information can be 
corrected through self-supervised graph completion, which recovers 
structural cues and strengthens feature consistency under noisy or 
partial observations (Che et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2023). Work on 
generative self-supervised reconstruction has also shown that corrupted 
or compromised data streams can be restored through denoising and 
structural recovery, leading to more reliable predictions in safety-
critical transportation settings (Li et al., 2025). Cyber-physical vision 
systems have also shown that attention-guided fusion can improve reli-
ability in safety-critical settings, supporting the need for stable feature 
aggregation under real-world noise (Hossain & Shorfuzzaman, 2025). 
Very few architectures jointly addressed multi-scale context modeling, 
fine-detail refinement, domain robustness, and real-time performance 
on constrained hardware. These persistent gaps motivated the design 
of IndiSegNet, which focuses on efficient multi-scale context capture, 
encoded feature refinement, and stable performance in unstructured 
and resource-limited environments.
3. Materials and methods
3.1. Materials
3.1.1. Dataset
We evaluated IndiSegNet on three benchmark datasets and a real-
world driving images. The Indian Driving Dataset (IDD) consists of 
10,003 images (6993 training, 981 validation, 2029 test) annotated 
across 26 classes and reflects unstructured traffic scenes, these classes 
include: Road, Sidewalk, Building, Wall, Fence, Pole, Traffic Light, 
Traffic Sign, Vegetation, Sky, Person, Rider, Car, Truck, Bus, Train, 
Motorcycle, Bicycle, Autorickshaw, Animal, Curb, Debris, Billboard, 
Bridge, and Miscellaneous objects. Cityscapes provides 5000 urban 
images (2975 training, 500 validation, 1525 test) with 30 semantic 
classes, captured from structured roadways in European cities. CamVid 
includes 700 frames (367 training, 101 validation, 233 test) with 32-
class annotations collected from UK urban driving videos. In addition, 
we collected and labeled a new driving dataset from diverse Indian 
locations, covering urban, rural, and mountainous scenes, to assess the 
geographic robustness of the proposed model. The IDD dataset differs 
considerably from Cityscapes and CamVid in terms of scene structure 
and visual complexity. Cityscapes contains highly organized European 
urban environments with well-marked lanes, consistent object layouts, 
and predictable traffic behavior. CamVid features simpler road scenes 
with low traffic density and minimal occlusion. In contrast, IDD cap-
tures unstructured Indian roads characterized by heterogeneous traffic 
(pedestrians, two-wheelers, auto-rickshaws, animals), irregular lane 
boundaries, highly cluttered backgrounds, varied lighting conditions, 
and frequent occlusions. These chaotic characteristics make IDD signif-
icantly more challenging and better suited for evaluating segmentation 
models in complex developing-region environments (see Fig. 1).
3.1.2. Data preprocessing
The raw images from IDD, Cityscapes, and CamVid contain varia-
tions in resolution, illumination, sensor noise, and dynamic range. To 
ensure uniformity during training, all images were resized to 256 × 256 
while preserving aspect ratio through padded scaling. Histogram nor-
malization was applied to reduce contrast imbalance, especially in 
IDD images captured under harsh lighting or monsoonal weather. We 
also removed corrupted or jittered frames to prevent unstable gradient 
updates.
3.1.3. Data augmentation
To improve generalization in unstructured and highly variable traf-
fic scenes, we employed several augmentation strategies. Random hori-
zontal flips, brightness–contrast jitter, and Gaussian noise were used to 
simulate diverse illumination conditions. Random cropping and scaling 
helped the network learn multi-scale object representations, especially 
for small classes such as pedestrians and motorcycles. Synthetic rain 
blur and partial occlusion masks were introduced to mimic common 
visibility challenges in Indian driving scenarios. These augmentations 
reduced overfitting and improved robustness to abrupt domain shifts.

3.2. Methods
The methods used in this study are grounded in encoder–decoder 
based semantic segmentation, where high-level contextual features are 
gradually refined through multi-scale fusion. We build upon standard 
convolutional backbones and enhance them with lightweight context-
capturing and feature-refinement principles to maintain both accuracy 
and computational efficiency.

based semantic segmentation principles, where a deep encoder extracts 
hierarchical feature representations and the decoder reconstructs dense 
pixel-wise predictions. Let 𝑋∈R𝐻×𝑊×3 denote an input image and 𝐹𝑙

IndiSegNet architecture (Section 4) is built. The MSCF and EFR modules 
extend these principles through efficient multi-scale context aggrega-
tion and feature refinement tailored for unstructured environments.
4. Proposed methodology
This section presents the proposed IndiSegNet architecture designed 
specifically for semantic segmentation in unstructured road environ-
ments. The fundamental challenge addressed here is to achieve accu-
rate pixel level understanding under large variations in object scale, 
irregular road geometry, illumination changes, and dynamic mixed 
traffic, while remaining computationally efficient for edge devices. The 
proposed methodology integrates multi-scale feature aggregation and 
progressive feature refinement to overcome these challenges. The goal 
is to build an architecture that maximizes contextual coverage while 
minimizing latency, enabling deployment on low-power autonomous 
driving platforms.
4.1. System model
The overall system follows an encoder–decoder formulation for 
dense prediction. Let 𝑋∈R𝐻×𝑊×3 denote an input RGB frame captured 
from the vehicle-mounted camera. The encoder extracts a hierarchy of 
feature maps {𝐹1, 𝐹2, 𝐹3, 𝐹4} at progressively lower resolutions, captur-
ing edges, textures, and semantic cues. These multi-scale features are 
then processed through the proposed MSCF modules to aggregate spa-
tial context, followed by the EFR modules for progressive refinement. 
The final output is a pixel-wise class prediction map ̂𝑌∈R𝐻×𝑊×𝐶
produced by a lightweight 1 × 1 convolution and softmax layer. This 
model supports real-time operation under the constraint of limited 
memory bandwidth and power availability.
4.2. Architecture and working of IndiSegNet
The network (Fig. 2) extracts feature maps at four stages from 
the backbone. These feature maps have dimensions of 128 × 128 × 64, 
64 × 64 × 256, 32 × 32 × 512, and 16 × 16 × 1024. Each feature map is 
upsampled by a factor of two.
The upsampled feature maps are passed to MSCF modules (Sec-
tion 4.2.1). MSCF1 processes the first and second tensors; MSCF2 
processes the second and third; MSCF3 processes the third and fourth. 
MSCF4 then fuses the outputs of MSCF2 and MSCF3; MSCF5 combines 
MSCF1 and MSCF2 outputs; MSCF6 integrates MSCF4 and MSCF5.
For global context integration, EFR modules (Section 4.2.2) are 
used. EFR1 fuses MSCF4 and MSCF6 outputs. EFR2 takes EFR1 and 
MSCF1 outputs. EFR3 fuses EFR2 with the first upsampled tensor. This 
final tensor has dimensions 256 × 256 × 64.
A 1 × 1 convolution followed by a softmax activation produces the 
final segmentation map, classifying each pixel into its corresponding 
category.
This approach ensures that the network effectively captures hierar-
chical feature representations, progressively refining local features and 
integrating global information to generate an accurate high-resolution 
segmentation output.

This module addresses scale adaptation and computation efficiency. 
It processes resolutions from 16 × 16 to 128 × 128, improving pedestrian 
detection at distances beyond 30 m by 9.2%. Each module adds only

The MSCF module enhances robustness by capturing contextual cues 
at multiple receptive fields, enabling the network to correctly interpret 
mixed-traffic layouts where small objects (pedestrians, motorcycles, 
animals) coexist with larger vehicles (buses, trucks) and where scene 
geometry is unpredictable. By fusing high-resolution and low-resolution 
features, MSCF improves recognition of distant or partially occluded 
objects and reduces ambiguity in visually chaotic regions.

Unstructured road environments, such as those in the IDD dataset, 
contain highly irregular object scales, heterogeneous traffic compo-
sition, weak lane structure, dense occlusions, and visually cluttered 
backgrounds. The proposed MSCF and EFR modules were designed 
specifically to address these challenges.
The EFR module further strengthens performance by progressively 
refining fused features to restore spatial detail that is often lost in 
downsampling. This refinement substantially improves boundary lo-
calization for thin or irregular structures such as road edges, poles, 
two-wheelers, and auto-rickshaws—elements that are difficult for con-
ventional encoder–decoder models to segment reliably in unstructured 
settings. EFR also improves separation of visually overlapping objects 
through local feature alignment.
Together, MSCF and EFR address the major sources of error in 
unstructured traffic: inconsistent lighting, heterogeneous object scales, 
occlusion, and background clutter. Their combined effect leads to more 
stable segmentation, higher mIoU for safety-critical classes, and im-
proved generalization across diverse and unpredictable road conditions.
5. Experiments, result and analysis
5.1. Experimental setup
We trained the network for 100 epochs using the Adam optimizer 
with a batch size of 8 and a learning rate of 0.001. The encoder 
was initialized with ImageNet-pretrained weights. Sparse categorical 
cross-entropy was used as the loss function. The buffer size was set to 
1000.

5.3.2. Performance on Cityscapes dataset
Table 2 and Fig. 8 compare IndiSegNet with other models on the

mIoU at 112 FPS. This is higher than ERFNet (70.0%), ESPNet (66.8%),

Table 3 and Figs. 9 and 10 show the performance of IndiSegNet 
on the CamVid dataset, demonstrating its ability to handle urban scene

FPS, showing a strong balance between accuracy and speed. It performs

Across the IDD, Cityscapes, and CamVid datasets, IndiSegNet consis-

on IDD in both Mean F1 Score and mIoU, showing its strength in

while maintaining real-time processing speed, and on CamVid, it shows 
solid performance in structured urban settings. Overall, IndiSegNet 
achieves a practical balance between accuracy and efficiency, making 
it suitable for real-time semantic segmentation in autonomous driving 
systems.
5.4. Analysis
This section presents a systematic analysis of IndiSegNet through a 
series of controlled experiments aimed at understanding the influence 
of architectural components on segmentation accuracy, computational 
efficiency, and deployment performance. By evaluating variations in 
backbone networks, filter configurations, context-aggregation modules, 
and decoder designs, we quantify how each choice affects the model’s 
ability to handle unstructured traffic scenes. The following subsections 
detail these investigations, providing empirical evidence that moti-
vates the final design of IndiSegNet and its suitability for real-time 
autonomous driving applications.
5.4.1. Experiment with different backbones
The choice of backbone significantly impacts both accuracy and ef-
ficiency in real-time systems. We compared ResNet50 with lightweight 
(MobileNetV3, ResNet18) and heavyweight (ResNet101) alternatives 
across mIoU, GFLOPs, latency, and power usage. As shown in Table 
4, ResNet50 offers a balanced trade-off. It achieves 78.9% mIoU on 
Cityscapes and 0.672 mIoU on IDD with only 3.8 GFLOPs, outper-
forming MobileNetV3 (0.61 mIoU on IDD) and running 2.1× faster 
than ResNet101. These results meet the ISO 26262 latency constraints 
(<50 ms) and are compatible with embedded platforms such as NVIDIA 
Xavier AGX (15 W).
To further optimize ResNet50 for unstructured environments, we 
applied two architectural changes. First, we replaced the initial 7 × 7 
convolution with three 3 × 3 layers, reducing memory usage by 18% 
while preserving the receptive field. Second, we reduced the expansion 
ratios in residual blocks, lowering FLOPs by 12% with less than 0.5% 
drop in mIoU. These improvements allow IndiSegNet to operate at 
112 FPS on Jetson Nano, satisfying both accuracy and deployment 
constraints.

Config 1 achieved the highest mIoU of 0.672, Although it has the 
most parameters, it achieves a higher FPS because its operations are 
structurally uniform and highly parallelizable. MSCF avoids dilation-
heavy or irregular fusion operations and maintains consistent feature-
map widths, leading to better memory locality and hardware utiliza-

larger parameter count. This shows that using consistent channel sizes 
helps preserve feature quality across scales, which is important for 
detecting small objects like pedestrians in busy scenes (Lei et al.,

parameters) but lost accuracy (mIoU dropped to 0.664). Its use of 
only 16 filters in MSCF1 limited low-level detail capture, which is 
needed for segmenting roads with unclear markings. Among the three 
tested settings, Configuration 1 was selected as the final operational 
configuration for IndiSegNet.

5.4.3. MSCF vs. SPP/ASPP context modules
To measure the benefit of our MSCF module’s hierarchical feature 
fusion, we replaced it with two commonly used alternatives: Spatial 
Pyramid Pooling (SPP) and Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling (ASPP). In 
both cases, the ResNet50 backbone and the EFR module were kept the 
same.
For SPP, we used pooling grids of {1 × 1, 2 × 2, 4 × 4, 8 × 8}, 
each followed by a 3 × 3 convolution with 64 filters. The outputs 
were concatenated and then reduced to 64 channels using a 1 × 1 
convolution. For ASPP, we used four parallel branches with atrous 
rates {6, 12, 18, 24}, each using a 3 × 3 convolution with 64 filters. 
The outputs from all branches were concatenated and projected to 64 
channels through a 1 × 1 convolution.
From Table 6, we observed that using SPP or ASPP instead of MSCF 
reduced the mIoU from 0.672 to 0.654 and 0.661, respectively, along 
with a 0.03 to 0.05 drop in Boundary F-Score. MSCF’s hierarchical 
fusion captured irregular object sizes more effectively, especially in 
unstructured traffic scenes. Although it ran 3 to 4 FPS slower, its 
lightweight convolutions were still more efficient than the dilated 
convolutions in ASPP or the large pooling layers in SPP.
5.4.4. EFR vs. UNet decoder
To test the effect of the EFR module, it was replaced with a standard 
UNet-style decoder. The ResNet50 backbone and MSCF module were 
kept unchanged. Only the decoding stage was modified. Each upsam-
pling step was performed using 2× nearest-neighbor resizing, followed 
by two 3 × 3 convolutions with 64 filters. Skip connections were added 
from the ResNet50 backbone at matching spatial resolutions.

5.4.5. Domain adaptation
Real-world deployment often exposes a segmentation model to en-
vironments whose visual characteristics differ substantially from the 
data on which the model was trained. To evaluate the robustness 
of IndiSegNet under such domain shift, we conduct a focused case 
study examining the transfer from structured Western urban scenes 
(Cityscapes) to the unstructured and visually heterogeneous Indian 
road environments present in the IDD dataset.
In the first experiment, IndiSegNet was trained exclusively on the 
Cityscapes dataset and then evaluated directly on the IDD validation 
split. As shown in Table 8, this ‘‘City → IDD’’ configuration achieved 
an mIoU of 0.482. While this score is significantly lower than the 0.661 
mIoU obtained by the model trained directly on IDD, it still exceeds 
the performance of several commonly used baselines when subjected 
to the same cross-domain setting. The results highlight the difficulty of 
transferring a model trained on clean and structured environments to 
the more chaotic and irregular scenes typical of Indian roads.
To understand whether simple appearance-level alignment can mit-
igate this performance degradation, we applied a lightweight his-
togram matching (HM) step. Each Cityscapes image was photometri-
cally aligned to the global color distribution of IDD before retraining 
IndiSegNet for 100 epochs. Without requiring any IDD annotations, 
this ‘‘City → IDD + HM’’ model improved mIoU to 0.533, recovering 
approximately 11% of the lost performance relative to the IDD-trained 
model. This demonstrates that a portion of the domain gap arises from 
low-level color and illumination differences.
5.5. Stress-test analysis under adverse conditions
IndiSegNet was tested under two common real-world challenges, 
heavy rain blur and partial occlusion. Failure rates were measured 
before and after applying mitigation techniques. All evaluations used 
the Bhubaneswar validation set (250 frames) with synthetically added 
distortions.
The following experiments analyze the robustness of IndiSegNet 
under adverse real-world conditions such as rain blur and partial 
occlusion. The techniques evaluated here, namely Weather-Adaptive 
Batch Normalization (WA-BN) and 5-frame temporal averaging, are not 
components of the final IndiSegNet architecture and are included solely 
as auxiliary stress-test analyses to expose performance degradation 
modes. 
For rain blur, a 15 × 15 Gaussian blur was applied to 30% of 
the frames to simulate heavy monsoon rainfall (Table 9). Without any 
mitigation, the average mIoU dropped from 0.661 to 0.567 (−0.094).

For partial occlusion, random regions measuring 20–50 pixels in

applied by averaging predictions across the current and four previous 
frames. This reduced the missed detection rate to 9%, increased pedes-

frames from 0.612 to 0.653, as shown in Table 10. These mitigation 
strategies are evaluated independently and are not incorporated into 
the final IndiSegNet model due to additional latency and deployment

not incorporated into the baseline IndiSegNet model. WA-BN slightly 
reduced accuracy on clean frames, and temporal averaging introduced 
additional latency and multi-frame dependence. As the baseline model 
is designed for high-speed, single-frame inference on edge hardware, 
these techniques are presented as optional robustness modules rather 
than core architectural elements.
6. Real-time use case: Hardware deployment and field testing
For real-world testing, the model was deployed using an ESP32 
Camera for video input and an Intel i7 13th-generation laptop (without 
a dedicated GPU) for processing, as shown in Fig. 12. The ESP32 
streamed video frames to the laptop over a network connection. All 
segmentation was performed on the CPU, showing that the model can 
run on systems with limited computational resources, an approach 
consistent with recent multi-source fusion strategies for sustainable 
edge computing (Cai et al., 2024). This setup allowed real-time eval-
uation of frame rate and segmentation quality in practical conditions.

6.1. Geographical robustness validation
To evaluate geographic robustness, IndiSegNet was tested in multi-
ple real-world environments across different regions. In Bhubaneswar, 
Odisha (Fig. 13), the model handled dense traffic, unmarked inter-
sections, and frequent rainfall during the monsoon. In Sikkim, along 
the Gangtok–Ravangla route (Fig. 14), it operated on steep gradients, 
sharp turns, and fog-prone mountain roads. In Durgapur, West Bengal, 
three different scenarios were tested: a semi-urban residential area in 
the Bidhannagar township (Fig. 15(c)), rural roads from Muchipara to 
Jaydev Kenduli with minimal lane markings (Fig. 15(a)), and a high-
speed highway segment (NH-19) between Durgapur and Asansol with 
mixed traffic (Fig. 15(b)).
Table 11 shows the evaluation results. Although IndiSegNet was 
deployed and tested in real-world environments, the accuracy metrics 
reported in Table 11 were computed only on manually annotated sam-
ples extracted from these test routes. A curated subset of frames from 
Bhubaneswar, Sikkim, and Durgapur was annotated using annotation 
tool to generate ground truth masks. These were then used to calculate 
mIoU, pedestrian recall. Despite changes in terrain and traffic patterns, 
IndiSegNet maintained consistent real-time segmentation performance. 
The mIoU varied by less than 2.5% (ranging from 0.661 to 0.677), 
pedestrian recall stayed above 97% across all routes, and inference 
speed remained above 108 FPS on the Jetson Nano. These results 
confirm that the model is ready for deployment in diverse traffic 
and environmental conditions. It is important to note that the real-
world regions used for evaluation in this subsection – Bhubaneswar 
(urban/monsoon), Sikkim (mountainous), and Durgapur (semi-urban 
and highway) – were not part of the training dataset.
7. Conclusion and future scope
IndiSegNet was presented as an edge-efficient semantic segmenta-
tion framework designed for complex and unstructured traffic environ-
ments. Built on a ResNet50 backbone and incorporating the proposed

MSCF and EFR modules, IndiSegNet achieved 0.781 mIoU on the IDD 
dataset while maintaining real-time inference at 112 FPS on the Jetson 
Nano. Field deployment across Sikkim, Durgapur, and Bhubaneswar 
confirmed stable performance under varied terrain and weather con-
ditions, with less than 2.5% variation in mIoU and pedestrian recall 
consistently above 97%. Under simulated heavy rain, weather-adaptive 
normalization recovered 0.062 mIoU without increasing latency. Oc-
clusion handling through five-frame temporal fusion improved mIoU 
by 0.041 and reduced the pedestrian miss rate from 23% to 9%, 
with only a minor 3 FPS reduction. Cross-domain testing showed that 
histogram matching improved Cityscapes-to-IDD transfer from 0.482 to 
0.533 mIoU without requiring IDD labels. The introduced ‘‘Safety Gain’’ 
metric (recall/ms) quantified the efficiency–accuracy balance, where 
IndiSegNet achieved 4.7%, outperforming heavier 3D convolutional 
models. Although IndiSegNet demonstrates strong performance, the 
study has certain limitations. The model relies solely on monocular 
RGB input, which reduces robustness under night-time or low-visibility 
conditions. The current design does not include explicit temporal mod-
eling, and the five-frame fusion strategy only partially compensates for 
motion-induced noise. Real-world testing was limited to three forward-
facing camera setups and daytime scenarios, leaving rare events and 
multi-view configurations unexplored. These constraints identify clear 
directions for enhancement in future work.
Future work will focus on integrating LiDAR data for multi-sensor 
fusion, incorporating ConvLSTM-based temporal modeling, and using 
neural architecture search for automated module optimization. IndiSeg-
Net demonstrates that lightweight, safety-aware segmentation networks 
can satisfy the real-time demands of diverse and resource-constrained 
autonomous systems.
